How I Work

Core Strengths

The toolkit I bring to every project I touch.

Product Strategy

I don't just build features. I build roadmaps that make sense for the business and the user. Every backlog item ties back to a measurable outcome.

Technical Operations

Coming from software management, I speak Engineer. I know how to manage the SDLC, coordinate QA cycles, and ship without getting lost in the weeds.

Behavioral Analytics

I care about how users actually behave, not how they say they will. KPI tracking, A/B testing, and behavioral data drive every product decision I make.

Operational Efficiency

If a process is manual and repetitive, I'm going to find a way to automate it. I've cut manual processing time by 40% across multiple projects.

← Back to Work

Consumer Platform

Community Engagement Engine

RoleProduct Manager
CompanyThe Brick TV
Timeline2022 - 2025
TypeZero-to-One Launch
The ProblemThe SolutionThe Impact

The Problem

The Brick TV is a culturally responsive streaming platform serving educators and community subscribers. We had great content, but users were siloed. There was no connective tissue to keep people sticking around or talking to one another. People watched and left. No structured way to capture what they wanted, no mechanism to turn their behavior into product decisions.

Subscriber engagement was flat. Onboarding was clunky across web, mobile, and TV. We were guessing at what content and features mattered instead of knowing.

The Solution

I led the launch of a new community feature that integrated directly with social platforms. Rather than guessing what users wanted, we built a feedback loop where social signals informed the product roadmap. I ran structured discovery conversations with educators, community members, and subscribers to surface usability gaps, then translated those findings into prioritized backlog items.

I designed subscriber onboarding workflows that reduced activation friction across web, mobile, and TV. I coordinated QA validation cycles before each release and maintained proactive communication with stakeholders throughout the platform lifecycle. Every feature improvement went through a clear process: identify the signal, write the user story, validate with acceptance criteria, ship, and measure.

The Impact

25%
Subscriber engagement improvement
20%
Retention improvement
40%
Post-launch defect reduction

We moved from guessing to knowing. By tracking engagement metrics and running A/B tests, we identified exactly which content areas and features subscribers valued most. That data drove every subsequent roadmap decision.

Next ProjectAutomated Reporting Dashboard
← Back to Work

Operational Tooling

Automated Reporting Dashboard

RoleProduct Manager
CompanySeneca Real Estate Services
Timeline2022 - 2025
TypeInternal Tooling
The ProblemThe SolutionThe Impact

The Problem

The team at Seneca Real Estate Services was buried in spreadsheets. Manual data entry was slowing down decisions, and by the time a report was ready, the data was already stale. Leadership had no real-time visibility into operational performance. Reporting errors were common because there was no standardized process for data entry or validation.

The Solution

I designed and deployed a centralized dashboard system that pulled fragmented data into a single view. The project started with a current-state workflow analysis where I mapped existing processes, identified bottlenecks, and documented the gaps between what teams needed and what the tools provided.

From there, I defined validation rules and standardized data entry processes to fix the data integrity issues at the source. I scoped automation requirements and delivered workflow enhancements through Agile sprint cycles, coordinating across internal teams and managing stakeholder expectations through each rollout phase.

The Impact

30%
Operational efficiency improvement
40%
Reduction in manual processing time

The manual labor bottleneck was gone. Stakeholders had real-time insights for the first time. The dashboard became the operational source of truth for leadership and program teams across both business units.

PreviousCommunity Engagement Engine
Next ProjectSocial-to-Strategy Research
← Back to Work

Market Intelligence

Social-to-Strategy Research

RoleProduct Manager
CompanyThe Brick TV
Timeline2022 - 2025
TypeResearch Framework
The ProblemThe SolutionThe Impact

The Problem

There was a disconnect between what our audience was saying on social platforms and what we were actually building. Educators and community members were posting feedback, sharing frustrations, and requesting features in spaces we weren't systematically monitoring. Every day, we were sitting on free market research and doing nothing with it.

The Solution

I built a framework to capture social sentiment and translate it into formal product requirements. The process worked in three layers: first, aggregate the signals from community channels and social platforms. Second, identify recurring patterns and cluster them into themes. Third, translate those themes into documented user personas, pain points, and prioritized backlog items.

This wasn't a one-time exercise. I embedded it into our ongoing discovery process so that community feedback continuously shaped the roadmap. Each cycle, the freshest signals got synthesized alongside behavioral data and subscriber feedback from direct conversations.

The Impact

20+
Backlog items directly sourced from social signal cycles over the platform lifecycle
Weekly
Cadence maintained across 3 years — community feedback continuously shaped the roadmap
20%
Retention improvement downstream — subscribers saw their feedback reflected in the product

The framework turned a passive social presence into an active intelligence layer. Running weekly cycles over three years meant the roadmap was never built on assumptions — every prioritization decision was traceable back to documented signals. Internal debate dropped sharply because opinions had to compete with evidence.

PreviousAutomated Reporting Dashboard
Next ProjectDual-View Project Dashboard

Project Management

Dual-View Project Dashboard

RoleProduct Manager
CompanySeneca Real Estate Services
Timeline2022 - 2025
TypeInternal Tooling
The ProblemThe SolutionThe Impact

The Problem

Stakeholders constantly asked for updates. Project leads had no real-time way to see if a project was on track or if the budget was burning too fast. Status conversations happened in meetings and Slack threads, not in a single source of truth. When overages surfaced, they were surprises, not managed risks.

The Solution

I built two integrated dashboards. The first was a Status Tracker that gave internal teams and external clients a clear view of task progression, from "Not Started" to "Completed," with visual progress indicators. The second was a Resource and Budget Monitor that compared planned hours against actual spend in real time.

Both views were designed to serve different audiences with the same underlying data. Project leads used the budget view to manage workloads proactively. Stakeholders and clients used the status view to stay informed without scheduling another meeting. I defined the requirements, coordinated the build through sprint cycles, and managed rollout and adoption across both business units.

The Impact

10+
Concurrent projects tracked simultaneously at peak across both business units
~50%
Reduction in ad-hoc status meeting and update requests after launch

Budget overages stopped being surprises. With real-time visibility into hours planned versus hours spent, project leads could adjust workloads before they became a problem — not after. The dashboard became the default source of truth for project status, cutting the volume of one-off update requests roughly in half and freeing leads to focus on delivery instead of reporting.

PreviousSocial-to-Strategy Research
Next ProjectEnterprise Government Implementation

Implementation Management

Enterprise Government Implementation Portfolio

RoleImplementation Manager
CompanySeneca Real Estate Services
Timeline2022 - 2025
TypeEnterprise SaaS
The ProblemThe SolutionThe Impact

The Problem

Seneca Real Estate Services was onboarding government agency clients onto CityVerse, a deeply configurable enterprise SaaS platform for right-of-way infrastructure management. There was no existing implementation process. No project templates, no onboarding frameworks, no escalation structure. Each new client arrived with different workflows, different approval chains, different data requirements, and different staff capacity. Leadership had no visibility into which contracts were on track and which were at risk. And at peak, ten-plus of these engagements were active simultaneously.

The Solution

I built every implementation process from scratch while running live client engagements at the same time. For each new contract I led a discovery session to understand how the agency actually operated before touching any configuration, then translated those requirements into a tailored platform setup including workflow logic, task hierarchies, form structures, and role-based access.

I built phased delivery plans with defined milestones and acceptance criteria at each stage. I created Power BI dashboards connected to live data so every client and internal stakeholder had real-time visibility into deployment health. When a Housing Authority director came to me frustrated mid-deployment with slow manual intake, I designed dynamic digital forms with automated routing inside the existing platform, going live without pausing the broader project timeline.

I trained 50+ staff across distributed sites, adapted delivery to each audience, and wrote SOPs that left every client equipped to operate independently post-launch.

The Impact

10+
Concurrent government implementations delivered on schedule
30%
Improvement in data accuracy across contract reporting
50+
Staff trained across distributed sites
0
Missed go-live deadlines

Total engagement lifecycles managed ranged from 6 months to 3 to 5 years. Every client launched on time with the documentation and training to operate independently.

IT Management

IT Infrastructure and Vendor Management Program

RoleIT Manager
CompanyGreenwood Seneca, LLC
Timeline2022 - 2025
TypeIT Operations
The ProblemThe SolutionThe Impact

The Problem

Greenwood Seneca was growing across two active subsidiaries simultaneously with no dedicated IT function. Software tools were being acquired reactively with no visibility into utilization, cost, or overlap. Hardware was being provisioned informally with no tracking system. There was no way to know which tools staff were actually using, what contracts were up for renewal, or what the organization's total technology spend looked like. Internal support requests came through three different channels with no routing, no tracking, and no resolution visibility.

The Solution

I built the entire IT management function from scratch. On the asset side, I implemented a barcode-based inventory tracking system for 120+ laptops, iPhones, and tablets, managing provisioning, configuration, shipping logistics, and full lifecycle records for every device.

On the vendor side, I became the primary point of contact for 5 to 10 active software vendor relationships, coordinating configurations and delivery timelines, conducting utilization reviews to evaluate platform fit, and recommending renewals or replacements based on actual usage data. I built a Power BI cost analysis dashboard tracking all technology spending across the organization, giving leadership real-time visibility into where money was going.

For internal support, I deployed Asana as a helpdesk ticketing system from zero. I designed routing rules, configured email-to-ticket automation, trained all staff, and brought the organization from no support structure to 50 to 75 tracked tickets per week. I also deployed Microsoft Copilot in a 6 to 8 week rollout, configuring cross-app workflows that eliminated 30 to 60 minutes of daily manual data processing.

The Impact

120+
Devices tracked through full lifecycle
50-75
Support tickets tracked per week
5hrs
Weekly manual work eliminated via Copilot

The technology spending dashboard gave leadership data-driven renewal and replacement decisions for the first time. Internal support went from zero visibility to a fully tracked, routed system with measurable improvement in resolution time.

PreviousEnterprise Government Implementation
Next ProjectNonprofit SaaS Consulting

Implementation Consulting

Nonprofit and Small Business SaaS Implementation Consulting

RoleImplementation Consultant
CompanyTargeted Business Solutions Inc.
Timeline2023 - Present
TypeConsulting
The ProblemThe SolutionThe Impact

The Problem

Small nonprofits and community-serving organizations are chronically under-resourced when it comes to technology. They often have the right tools available to them but no one with the expertise to configure them properly, migrate their data, or train their teams in a way that sticks. The result is platforms that get purchased and never fully adopted, grant reporting that still happens in spreadsheets, and staff who revert to manual processes because the platform feels harder than the workaround.

I started taking on consulting engagements specifically because I had watched this pattern across multiple organizations and knew what it cost them.

The Solution

Across 5+ client engagements I managed the full implementation cycle from discovery through go-live and post-launch support. Every engagement started the same way: I spent time understanding how the organization actually operated before recommending or touching any configuration.

For nonprofit clients I built grant reporting infrastructure directly integrated into their existing funding cycles, delivering board-ready Power BI dashboards and clean datasets that supported compliance documentation and funding applications. For clients managing community programs I designed workflow automation that eliminated manual hand-offs between staff.

For every client I built plain-language training materials and self-service documentation designed for organizations where staff turnover is high and institutional knowledge walks out the door. The goal was always the same: leave each organization genuinely more capable, not dependent on me coming back.

The Impact

5+
Organizations served across nonprofits and small businesses
100%
Client go-live rate — every engagement delivered on time and fully operational

Post-launch support volume reduced across all engagements through self-service documentation. Multiple clients successfully used the reporting infrastructure built during engagements to support grant applications and board reporting.

Next ProjectAI and Automation Deployment

AI and Automation

Internal AI and Automation Deployment Program

RoleIT Manager / Implementation Lead
CompanyGreenwood Seneca, LLC
Timeline2022 - 2025
TypeAI/Automation
The ProblemThe SolutionThe Impact

The Problem

Greenwood Seneca was operating two active subsidiaries with a lean team and no room for manual inefficiency. Data from CityVerse was being manually transferred to Power BI every day, 30 to 60 minutes of work that produced reports that were already outdated by the time anyone read them. Internal support requests had no system, no routing, and no visibility. Communications to stakeholders, partners, and subscribers were being managed manually with no CRM and no consistency. The organization needed the operational capacity of a much larger team without adding headcount.

The Solution

I identified three separate automation and AI deployments that each addressed a distinct operational bottleneck.

For Microsoft Copilot, I scoped the problem first, then configured cross-application workflows that connected CityVerse and Power BI directly. The deployment took 6 to 8 weeks including configuration, testing, and full staff rollout.

For Asana, I built a complete internal helpdesk from scratch: designed ticket routing rules, configured an email-to-ticket automation pipeline so requests logged automatically regardless of how staff submitted them, and trained the full organization through adoption. We went from zero structure to 50 to 75 tracked and routed tickets per week.

For HubSpot, I built the CRM database and newsletter infrastructure from scratch across all three Greenwood Seneca organizations, configuring templates, contact management, and weekly distribution workflows for both internal and external communications.

Each deployment followed the same discipline: define the workflow problem first, configure to solve that problem specifically, train through the change, and measure what improved.

The Impact

5hrs
Weekly manual work eliminated via Copilot
50-75
Weekly tickets tracked through Asana helpdesk
3
Automation deployments built and operational in 6-8 weeks each

Microsoft Copilot delivered real-time dashboards to government clients instead of day-old exports. Asana brought the organization from zero support visibility to a fully tracked, routed, and auditable support system. HubSpot replaced manual contact management with a structured CRM and consistent communication cadence across three organizations.

PreviousNonprofit SaaS Consulting

About

Product Manager. Implementation Lead. IT Builder. Bias toward outcomes.

I'm Darryl Beatty. Over the past 8+ years I've worked across product management, SaaS implementation, and IT operations. At Greenwood Seneca I wore all three hats simultaneously: managing the product roadmap for The Brick TV, a culturally responsive streaming platform; leading 10+ concurrent enterprise implementations for government agencies on CityVerse; and building the entire IT function from scratch across two active subsidiaries. Before that, I spent nearly five years in QA and business analysis roles at Diversified Media Group.

That range is the point. I've been the person writing the user stories, the person configuring the platform, the person training the end users, and the person tracking whether any of it actually worked. I push for clear requirements because I've seen what happens when they're vague. I measure outcomes because shipping a feature or launching a deployment means nothing if nobody uses it.

I'm currently looking for my next role in Product Management, Implementation Management, or IT leadership. If you need someone who can operate across all three and deliver results in each, I'd like to hear about it.
